topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of nervous energy and carefully curated introspection into, now displayed in bold, perfect type on a university admissions website. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet library corner. I traced the elegant sentence about navigating uncertainty, the one that felt so authentically *me*, now sitting beside a thousand other polished narratives. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a heavy, suffocating pressure. I quickly shoved the paper into my backpack, my fingers trembling as I zipped the compartment shut. I had to leave. Now. The weight of that published self felt unbearable.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the final paragraph of my personal statement. It was there, perfectly formatted, a ghost of my own voice, now belonging to some other hopeful applicant. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. My hands started to shake, and I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady them. I felt a profound sense of trespass, as if I had violated some sacred boundary. I wanted to snatch the document back, to erase the digital footprint, but my movements were sluggish, paralyzed by a sudden wave of shame. I leaned back, the cheap plastic chair groaning under my sudden stillness, and let the silence of the room amplify the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 3]

It happened while I was scrolling through a recommendation aggregator. There, nestled between glowing testimonials, was my essay. It wasn't just posted; it was presented as a model, an exemplar. A slow, sickening lurch took hold of my core. I felt my cheeks burn, a deep, internal heat that radiated outward. I looked down at my hands, noticing the faint tremor that had started in my fingertips. Every instinct screamed at me to delete the browser history, to vanish. I couldn't look away, though; I just kept staring at the text, the words mocking the effort I had put in, now commodified and displayed for consumption. I stood up abruptly, the sound of my chair scraping the floor sounding deafeningly loud in the sudden emptiness of the room.
